title: Tuning J$_1$-J$_2$ in Quasi-2D Triangular Lattice Antiferromagnet $α$-SrCr$_2$O$_4$ via Uniaxial Pressure
authors: Jazzmin Victorin, Shreenanda Ghosh, Seyed Koohpayeh, Chris Lygouras, Natalia Drichko
abstract: Triangular lattice antiferromagnets first attracted attention as a frustrated magnetic lattice which can serve as a platform to realize the resonating valence bond state. While the triangular lattice itself was shown to support classical 120 degree order, many theoretical phase diagrams suggest a quantum spin liquid state within a small range of parameters. One possible avenue to achieve such a state is to tune the anisotropy of the triangular lattice antiferromagnet by applying uniaxial pressure. This motivated our Raman scattering study of quasi-two-dimensional antiferromagnet $α$-SrCr$_2$O$_4$ under applied uniaxial pressure. Under ambient conditions, $α$-SrCr$_2$O$_4$ develops long-range helical magnetic order below T$_N$ = 43 K. We identify two-magnon excitations associated with this long-range antiferromagnetic order below T$_N$ at 15.5 meV and 40 meV by comparison with spin wave calculations. We observe the two features from the two-magnon excitation shift away from (towards) each other under applied tensile (compressive) pressure, indicating a decrease (increase) in anisotropy. Raman active phonons show a shift to higher (lower) frequencies under applied compressive (tensile) pressure, indicating efficient transmission of pressure and tuning of the lattice. We show spin wave and two-magnon density of states calculations under uniaxial pressure are consistent with our experimental results.
summary: This paper explores how applying uniaxial pressure can tune the magnetic frustration and anisotropy in the triangular lattice antiferromagnet alpha-SrCr2O4. By using Raman spectroscopy, the authors show that mechanical strain can shift magnetic excitation energies, providing a method to control the magnetic ground state. This work is significant for understanding how lattice distortions influence quantum magnetic phases.
main_problem: Investigating the ability to tune the magnetic anisotropy and exchange interaction ratio (J2/J1) in a frustrated triangular lattice antiferromagnet using in-situ uniaxial pressure.
main_result: The study demonstrates that compressive pressure reduces magnetic anisotropy, while tensile pressure increases it, effectively tuning the frustration in the system. The researchers also confirmed efficient pressure transmission through shifts in Raman-active phonon frequencies.
method: In-situ Raman spectroscopy was used under applied uniaxial strain via a piezoelectric cell, complemented by Linear Spin Wave Theory (LSWT) and two-magnon density of states calculations.
model_or_system: The system is alpha-SrCr2O4, a quasi-2D S=3/2 triangular lattice Heisenberg antiferromagnet with an orthorhombic structure and incommensurate helical magnetic order below 43 K.
key_observables: Two-magnon excitations (at 15.5 meV and 40 meV), Raman-active oxygen phonon frequencies, phonon line shapes (Fano asymmetry), and Gruneisen parameters.
important_parameters: Exchange anisotropy ratio (J2/J1), Neel temperature (TN = 43 K), strain range (-0.41% to +0.16%), and Gruneisen parameter (gamma = 0.45).
main_assumptions: Assumed a Poisson ratio of 0.2 and that phonon width temperature dependence follows the Klemens model; used the approximation of Linear Spin Wave Theory for magnon dispersion.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Crystal structure and exchange paths; Fig 2: Schematic of strain application; Fig 3: Temperature dependence of phonons in the strain cell; Fig 4: Raman spectra under different pressures; Fig 5: Temperature-dependent Raman spectra; Fig 7: LSWT calculations for spin wave dispersion and two-magnon DOS.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the frustrated triangular lattice problem, describes the experimental setup for uniaxial pressure Raman spectroscopy, presents the results of phonon frequency shifts to verify strain, details the observation of two-magnon excitation tuning, and concludes with theoretical spin-wave modeling to interpret the magnetic anisotropy changes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Schematic of CS100 Razorbill strain cell and optical microscope image of bar-shaped α-SrCr2O4 sample used for uniaxial pressure measurements.

FIG. 1. (a) Crystal structure of quasi-2D TLHAF α-SrCr2O4 out-of-plane. Light and dark blue spheres denote two in- equivalent Cr3+ sites. (b) The bc plane of α-SrCr2O4 with Sr atoms omitted for clarity. (c) Simplified schematic view of exchange interactions on triangular lattice of Cr3+ ions. Val- ues in schematic in units of meV.

FIG. 3. (a) Temperature dependence of α-SrCr2O4 sam- ple mounted in a strain cell in the spectral range of oxygen phonons between 400 and 650 cm−1. Unpolarized Raman spectra shifted along the y axis for clarity. (b) Frequencies of select oxygen-related phonons marked in panel (a). Solid lines are fits to symmetrical three-phonon coupling model (Eq. 3).

FIG. 4. (a) Unpolarized Raman spectra of α-SrCr2O4 at 35 K in the strain cell at frequencies above 400 cm−1 at maxi- mum tensile pressure, zero pressure, and maximum compres- sive pressure. Inset: Ag 602 cm−1 phonon. (b) Peak Fre- quency of Ag 602 cm−1 phonon as a function of applied uni- axial pressure

FIG. 5. (a) Unpolarized temperature dependent Raman spec- tra of α-SrCr2O4 at frequencies below 400 cm−1 in the strain cell. Spectra shifted along the y axis for clarity. (b) Frequen- cies of select oxygen-related phonons marked in panel (a). (c) Temperature dependence of the electron-phonon coupling strength, 1/q, of Ag 102 cm−1 phonon.
